title: Friday Abstinence
summary: The universal Friday penance honoring Our Lord's Passion.
history: From the earliest centuries, Christians fasted on Fridays — the day of Our Lord's crucifixion. The Didache (c. 70 AD) prescribes Wednesday and Friday fasting. For most of Church history, all Fridays required complete abstinence from meat. In 1966, Pope Paul VI's apostolic constitution Paenitemini allowed episcopal conferences to substitute other forms of penance for Friday abstinence outside of Lent. However, this was a permission, not a command — the traditional practice of Friday abstinence year-round remains fully valid and meritorious.
explanation: Abstinence means refraining from eating meat (flesh of warm-blooded animals). Fish, eggs, dairy, and shellfish are permitted. This discipline reminds us weekly of Christ's sacrifice on the Cross and unites our small mortification with His suffering. Traditional Catholics maintain this practice every Friday of the year, not only during Lent, as it was the universal law of the Church for nearly two millennia.
practice: Abstain from meat every Friday. This includes beef, pork, chicken, and other flesh meat. Fish, eggs, cheese, and dairy are permitted. Soups, broths, and gravies made from meat are also abstained from. The obligation binds from age 14. If a Solemnity falls on a Friday (e.g., Christmas), abstinence is not required.
churchTeaching: 1917 Code of Canon Law, Canon 1250: 'Days of abstinence are all Fridays of the year.' Pope Benedict XIV, Non Ambigimus (1741), detailed the laws of fasting and abstinence.

title: The Lenten Fast
summary: Forty days of penance in preparation for Easter.
history: Lent has been observed since at least the Council of Nicaea (325 AD). The forty-day fast mirrors Christ's forty days in the desert (Mt 4:1-2), Moses's forty days on Sinai (Ex 34:28), and Elijah's forty-day journey to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8). The traditional Lenten discipline was rigorous: one full meal per day with two smaller 'collations' that together did not equal the main meal, and complete abstinence from meat on all days. The fast began on Ash Wednesday and continued through Holy Saturday.
explanation: The traditional Lenten fast has two components: fasting (restricting the amount of food) and abstinence (refraining from meat). Under the traditional discipline: all weekdays of Lent are days of fasting, and all Fridays and Saturdays of Lent are days of abstinence. Sundays are never fast days (they celebrate the Resurrection) but some traditional Catholics maintain abstinence even on Lenten Sundays. The fast is broken by eating between meals.
practice: Fasting: One full meal and two smaller collations (which together should not equal a full meal). No eating between meals. Liquids (water, coffee, tea) are permitted between meals. Abstinence: No meat on Ash Wednesday, all Fridays of Lent, and (traditionally) all Saturdays of Lent. The fast binds from age 21 to 59; abstinence from age 14. Additional Lenten practices: Stations of the Cross on Fridays, increased almsgiving, giving up a lawful pleasure, daily Mass attendance.
churchTeaching: Pope Benedict XIV, Non Ambigimus (1741). 1917 Code of Canon Law, Canons 1250-1254.

title: Ember Days
summary: Seasonal days of fasting and prayer for the sanctification of the clergy.
history: The Ember Days (Latin: Quatuor Anni Tempora, 'Four Seasons of the Year') are among the most ancient observances in the Roman Calendar, dating to at least the 5th century under Pope St. Leo the Great. They occur four times per year, roughly at the start of each season: in Advent (after the third Sunday), in Lent (after the first Sunday), in the Pentecost octave, and in September (after the Exaltation of the Holy Cross). Traditionally, ordinations to the priesthood were conferred on the Saturdays of Ember Weeks.
explanation: Each set of Ember Days consists of Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday. Wednesday and Saturday are days of fasting and partial abstinence; Friday is a day of fasting and complete abstinence. The Ember Days serve multiple purposes: thanksgiving for the harvest, petition for God's blessing on the coming season, prayer for priestly vocations and the sanctification of the clergy, and general penance. The 1960 rubrical reform suppressed the Ember Days from the general calendar, but they remain in the pre-1960 calendar.
practice: In the 1962 calendar, Ember Days are no longer obligatory but remain in the calendar as days of recommended penance. In the pre-1955 and 1955 calendars, they retain their full force. Traditional Catholics who wish to observe them: fast on all three days, abstain completely on Friday, and offer prayers for priestly vocations. The specific dates vary each year based on the liturgical calendar.
churchTeaching: Pope St. Leo the Great, Sermon 19: 'The fasts of the four seasons are so distributed over the whole year that in every season the law of abstinence is before us.'

title: Rogation Days
summary: Days of public prayer and procession beseeching God's protection.
history: The Major Rogation (April 25) predates Christianity — it replaced the pagan Roman Robigalia, a festival asking for protection of crops from blight. Pope St. Gregory the Great Christianized it as a day of litanies and procession. The Minor Rogations (Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday before Ascension Thursday) were instituted by St. Mamertus, Bishop of Vienne, around 470 AD, after a series of earthquakes, fires, and wild animal attacks afflicted his diocese. The people processed through the fields singing the Litany of the Saints, and the calamities ceased.
explanation: The Rogation Days are days of public supplication — 'rogare' means 'to ask.' The Litany of the Saints is chanted in procession, and Mass is offered with special prayers for God's blessing on crops, protection from natural disasters, and the spiritual welfare of the community. The Major Rogation (April 25) is a day of fasting; the Minor Rogations are days of fasting in the pre-1960 calendar.
practice: Where possible, join the parish Rogation Day procession with the Litany of the Saints. If no procession is available, pray the Litany of the Saints privately. Fast if following the pre-1960 calendar. Pray for God's protection over your community and the fruits of the earth. These days are a powerful reminder that we depend entirely on God's providence for our daily bread.
